claim: TAP is the inverse of existing accelerators: top-down (leadership sets system priorities, then assembles entity capacity), not bottom-up grassroots support.

evidence.detail: lean joint programme led by a group of entities; offer surge capacity

claim: The UN has abundant entity-level implementation capacity but very little system-level implementation capacity; TAP attaches to entity capacity rather than building a new system-level team.

evidence.detail: only 32/114 explicit core-innovation roles; capacity embedded in programme/digital/data

caveat: Corroborate with CEB Innovation Capacity Assessment (2024).

claim: Pooled funding works at UN system scale; CRAF'd is the named benchmark.

evidence.detail: >$40M committed; ~$12bn crisis funding shaped; 35+ partners + 120 implementing orgs; MPTF Office = Administrative Agent
scale pooled funding building on CRAF'd

claim: Inter-agency pooled funding is a proven, growing channel with political momentum.

evidence.detail: $1.1bn transferred 2024; 71 contributors; pooled = 8.1% of earmarked dev (2023); Funding Compact target 30% non-core via pooled by 2027

claim: WFP Innovation Accelerator is the demand-signal benchmark; its scale shows what good bottom-up demand generation looks like.

evidence.detail: 2024: 61M people reached, $323M catalysed; since 2015: 62 bootcamps, 519 teams, 500+ ventures across 90+ countries

claim: Equity-free, open-source pooled investment is an established UN funding precedent (UNICEF Venture Fund).

evidence.detail: $35M+ pooled fund (first of its kind in UN); equity-free; 153 solutions / 80+ countries; 174M reached; portfolio raised 12x initial capital

claim: The 2025 funding crunch is severe and sharpens TAP's pool-to-survive case.

evidence.detail: >30% of global humanitarian funding lost 2024->2025; US ~$14bn->$3.7bn; USAID shut; UNHCR -5,000 posts, WHO -2,300, WFP -6,000

claim: The ICT fragmentation TAP attacks is quantified; TAP is funded by reinvesting consolidation savings.

evidence.detail: >$2bn/yr on basic software/cloud/connectivity in isolation; TAP funded by reinvesting savings from consolidation under common providers (UNICC)

claim: Data Commons is the flagship proof case of the TAP top-down loop (leadership commitment first, then build).

evidence.detail: UN system data commons; federated; collective financing building on CRAF'd

caveat: Confirm headcount/entity count via direct Data Commons interview.

claim: Digital ID is the proof case of 'small technical owner + institutional (HLCM) mandate' working over years.

evidence.detail: interagency success story tied to HLCM; technical owner not branded to host entity

caveat: Confirm via direct interview; verify current contract host.

claim: TAP Business Needs Survey percentages are internal WIP, not citable.

evidence.detail: survey method weaker than bottom-up demand generation; answers vary by respondent group

caveat: Validate raw survey + methodology before any percentage is published.

claim: Futures Lab under-invested in technical capacity (a TAP staffing lesson, not the failure cause).

evidence.detail: staffing skewed to comms/partnerships/admin over technical roles

caveat: Single-source recollection; funding cut was the proximate cause. Treat as staffing lesson.
